text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a robotic ultra-low anterior, essentially a coloanal, for a low T-turectal cancer. This was at about six centimeters from the anal verge. I have placed an ICG tattoo there because it was a small lesion and did not expect it to be visible from the outside. as well as the fact that I usually do partial TMEs, tumor-specific mesorectal excisions and not always a total. So if I could preserve a little rectum, that's what I was gonna do. I'm highlighting in this case just the steps of TME or the process of a TME and not necessarily all the steps of the case. I did forget to turn the recording back on after I had placed my anvil so you don't see the anastomosis, but I can assure you that for a stapled anastomosis, I guess it was as good as you could get. I will do stapled anastomosis when there is nothing that I can sew to. And I would prefer to do a stapled anastomosis when possible in a local anal compared to a sewn coloanal.

The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using a vessel sealer, creating a window for access. The procedure continues with lateral dissection to identify the left-sided plane, providing a clear view around the mesorectum for further dissection.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection in the holy plane, marked by the ICG tattoo, using monopolar cautery. The dissection progresses to the right lateral side, connecting it to the anterior and left lateral dissection. The wider pelvis facilitates the procedure, allowing the use of monopolar scissors without the need for bipolar or vessel sealer.

The surgeon enters the rectovaginal septum, carefully dissecting the mesorectum while preserving the vagina. The uterus is suspended to the abdominal wall with a Keith needle for deeper exposure. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the mesorectum, aiming to achieve two to three centimeters beneath the ICG tattoos.

The surgeon continues the circumferential dissection around the mesorectum, ensuring to stay even and avoid creating holes. The dissection progresses below the ICG tattoos, which are positioned at the tumor level. The levator muscles are visible beneath, as the mesorectum is lifted away from them.

The surgeon uses four ports without an assist, switching the camera to arm number two for a straight view into the deep pelvis. The levator muscles are carefully detached from the mesorectum attachments, ensuring the dissection is below the tumor. The specimen is extracted through the ileostomy site to minimize hernia risk, and the mesorectum is fully removed, preparing for a coloanal anastomosis flush with the levators.

The surgeon uses a 45mm stapler to transect the rectum, aiming from anterior to posterior, while ensuring the vagina is not accidentally included. The proximal mesentery is taken up to the cephalad aspect of the inferior mesenteric artery, marking the chosen proximal margin in the proximal sigmoid colon. The surgeon marks the planned margin with cautery for exteriorization and placement of an anvil. The transected rectum is visible in the levator crease.
